title: Treasury Releases Report Showing U.S. Childcare System Overburdens Families and Causes Shortages Due to Inadequate Supply
summary: The U.S. Treasury released a report on September 15, 2021 documenting how the child care market fails families due to insufficient supply and liquidity constraints. Secretary Yellen and Vice President Harris promoted the Build Back Better agenda as the solution, which would expand universal preschool and subsidize child care costs.
key_points: Published September 15, 2021 – over 4.5 years before TODAY (2026-04-02); classified as historical record
Report authored by acting Assistant Secretary for Economic Policy Catherine Wolfram in the Office of Economic Policy
Average family with child under 5 spends approximately 13% of income on childcare; Build Back Better proposal aims to reduce to 7%
Report identifies market failures: liquidity constraints and positive externalities (spillover benefits of early education)
Policy proposals include universal preschool for 3- and 4-year-olds and expanded Child and Dependent Care Tax Credit
No direct relevance to market structure or macro catalysts as defined for this archive
why_it_matters: This source is a domestic fiscal policy announcement about childcare subsidies, not a macro catalyst relevant to financial market analysis. It discusses labor market participation and economic growth arguments but does not address monetary policy, interest rates, Treasury market operations, or economic data releases that typically move markets.
important_numbers: 7% of family income (proposed target under Build Back Better)
